title: Polio case and death rates in the United States
subtitle: The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both [wild-](#dod:wild-poliovirus) and [vaccine-derived poliovirus](#dod:vaccine-derived-poliovirus) infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.
caption:
Polio case and death rates in the United States

The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both wild- and vaccine-derived poliovirus infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.

This line chart tracks the polio case rate and polio death rate in the United States from 1910 to 2023, with values measured per 100,000 population. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 40. The polio case rate, represented by a brown line, shows significant fluctuation in the first half of the century. It begins near 7 in 1910, spikes to approximately 28 around 1916, and drops back below 5 by 1920. Between 1920 and 1940, the rate oscillates mostly between 2 and 13. A period of sustained increase begins in the early 1940s, culminating in the chart's highest peak around 1952, where the case rate exceeds 35. Following this peak, the line descends sharply, falling below 5 by 1960 and reaching zero shortly thereafter, where it remains flat through 2023. The polio death rate, shown as a blue line, follows a similar trajectory but at a much lower magnitude. It peaks around 1916 near 7 and again in the early 1950s at approximately 2. Like the case rate, the death rate drops to near zero by 1960 and stays at the baseline for the remainder of the timeline. Both lines converge at zero from the 1960s onward, indicating no reported cases or deaths per 100,000 population in the latter decades of the chart.
